name: Katana
tagline: A fast Go crawler that maps every URL, endpoint, and JS file on a target site
seoTitle: Katana - Fast Web Crawler & Spidering CLI
metaDescription: Katana is a Go command-line web crawler from ProjectDiscovery. It discovers URLs, endpoints, and JavaScript files using standard or headless crawling.
overview: Katana is a command-line crawling and spidering framework written in Go, built by ProjectDiscovery. You point it at a URL or a list of URLs, and it walks the site to discover every reachable URL, endpoint, and JavaScript file.

It is aimed at security testers, bug bounty hunters, and engineers who need to map a site's attack surface or build automation pipelines. Katana runs in a standard HTTP mode or a headless (real browser) mode, and can parse JavaScript files to find endpoints that a plain crawler would miss.

Within the web scraping and crawling category, Katana focuses on speed and scriptability. It reads input from STDIN, a single URL, or a file list, and writes output to STDOUT, a file, or JSON, so it fits cleanly into shell pipelines alongside other tools.
features: Standard and headless (real browser) crawling modes
JavaScript parsing and crawling to surface hidden endpoints
Scope control via preconfigured fields or custom regex
Flexible input (STDIN, URL, list) and output (STDOUT, file, JSON)
Configurable crawl depth, duration, and per-domain page limits

gettingStarted.intro: Katana requires Go 1.26+ to install from source, or you can pull a prebuilt Docker image. Once installed, you can crawl a target with a single command.

steps.heading: Install with Go
steps.body: Install the latest Katana binary using the Go toolchain. CGO must be enabled.

steps.heading: Or pull the Docker image
steps.body: If you prefer not to build from source, pull the official image.

steps.heading: Crawl a target
steps.body: Pass a URL with -u to start a standard crawl. Add -headless for browser-based crawling.

steps.heading: Check the available flags
steps.body: Run the help command to see all supported switches, including depth, JS crawling, and scope control.

useCases: Mapping a web application's URLs and endpoints during a security assessment or bug bounty
Extracting endpoints from JavaScript files that a basic crawler would miss
Feeding discovered URLs into automation pipelines via STDIN/STDOUT and JSON output
Crawling single-page apps and JS-heavy sites in headless mode with a real browser
